items.q: If a car doubles its speed, what happens to its kinetic energy?
items.correct: It quadruples (because KE = 1/2 mv^2 — kinetic energy is proportional to v^2).
items.misconception: Kinetic energy doubles because energy is proportional to speed.
items.random_wrong: Kinetic energy stays the same because mass hasn't changed.
items.why: Students assume linear relationships (double the input, double the output) and forget the v^2 factor in the kinetic energy formula.

items.q: Is the internal energy of a system the same as the heat it contains?
items.correct: No — a system doesn't 'contain heat'; internal energy is the total kinetic and potential energy of its particles, changed by heating or doing work.
items.misconception: Yes — internal energy is just the stored heat in the object.
items.random_wrong: No — internal energy refers only to the chemical potential energy.
items.why: Students treat heat as a substance stored in objects rather than a process of energy transfer, blurring the heat/internal energy distinction.

items.q: What determines the energy of a single photon?
items.correct: Its frequency (E = hf) — only frequency, not intensity.
items.misconception: A brighter light has higher-energy photons because intensity makes each photon more energetic.
items.random_wrong: Photon energy depends on how fast the photon is moving relative to the source.
items.why: Students conflate intensity (number of photons) with photon energy (frequency-dependent); this is why dim UV is more dangerous than bright red.
It's Slows neutrons. “Speeds neutrons” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's H to He. “Heavy nuclei” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximate energy per ²³⁵U fission in MeV:
items.why: The real figure is 200. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
